operating_under_the_influence.title: OUI / DUI Traffic Stop
operating_under_the_influence.description: Navigating a high-pressure traffic stop, roadside questioning, Field Sobriety Tests (FSTs), and breathalyzer choices.

start.title: Phase 1: The Pull Over & Opening Question
start.description: You are pulled over late at night. The officer shines a flashlight in your window and asks: 'Do you know why I pulled you over? Have you had anything to drink tonight?'

choices.text: Hand over your license/registration and say: 'I decline to answer any questions without an attorney.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: You provide required driving documents but invoke your 5th Amendment right against self-incrimination immediately. You cannot be forced to answer investigatory questions.

choices.text: Say: 'No, officer, I haven't had a single drop.'
choices.feedback: SAFE BUT RISKY: If you truly haven't drank, this is fine. If you have, lying to an officer can be used against you later to prove consciousness of guilt.

choices.text: Say: 'I just had one or two beers a few hours ago.'
choices.feedback: WAIVER: You just admitted to consuming alcohol. This gives the officer instant Probable Cause to extend the stop and investigate you for OUI.

choices.text: Refuse to roll down the window or hand over your license.
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL: Under MA law, you must provide license and registration during a lawful traffic stop. Refusing is an arrestable offense independent of the OUI.

the_exit_order.title: Phase 2: The Exit Order
the_exit_order.description: The officer says, 'I smell alcohol. Step out of the vehicle.'

choices.text: Turn off the car, take your keys, step out safely, and lock the door behind you.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Under Pennsylvania v. Mimms, police can order you out of the car. Locking it behind you prevents an unconstitutional warrantless search of your vehicle.

choices.text: Step out but leave the door wide open and the engine running.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: You complied with the exit order, but leaving the door open gives officers 'plain view' access to look inside your car.

choices.text: Argue: 'You don't have a warrant, I'm staying in my car.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: The Supreme Court ruled officers do not need a warrant to order occupants out during a lawful stop. Refusing escalates to physical extraction.

choices.text: Put the car in drive and speed away.
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL: Fleeing constitutes Evading Police / Failure to Stop, turning a potential misdemeanor into a serious felony pursuit.

field_sobriety_tests.title: Phase 3: Field Sobriety Tests (FSTs)
field_sobriety_tests.description: The officer points his flashlight at the ground. 'I need to make sure you're safe to drive. Follow my pen with your eyes, then walk this line heel-to-toe.'

choices.text: Politely state: 'I respectfully decline to perform any physical tests.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: In Massachusetts, FSTs are 100% voluntary. Refusing them deprives the state of subjective 'evidence' of impairment (like stumbling).

choices.text: Say: 'I have a bad knee, can we do a different test?'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: While establishing a medical baseline is okay, engaging in *any* FST usually provides evidence for the officer to justify an arrest.

choices.text: Attempt the tests to prove you are sober.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: FSTs are designed to be failed. Even sober people fail under the stress, flashing lights, and uneven pavement.

choices.text: Start doing jumping jacks to show off your coordination.
choices.feedback: DANGEROUS: Erratic behavior will be documented as 'extreme intoxication' and 'tumultuous behavior', guaranteeing your arrest.

portable_breathalyzer.title: Phase 4: The Portable Breath Test (PBT)
portable_breathalyzer.description: Since you refused the physical tests, the officer pulls out a handheld breathalyzer. 'Just blow into this real quick so we can clear this up.'

choices.text: State: 'I refuse to take the roadside breath test.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: In MA, refusing a *roadside* PBT does NOT suspend your license. It deprives them of the probable cause needed to arrest you.

choices.text: Ask: 'Will I lose my license if I refuse this specific test?'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: A good question, but officers may use confusing language to make you think you have to take it. Best to just decline.

choices.text: Blow into the device hoping you are under 0.08.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: If it reads even slightly positive, they will use it as Probable Cause to arrest you, even if it's under the legal limit.

choices.text: Grab the device out of the officer's hand.
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL: Assault on a Police Officer and Destruction of Evidence. You will be immediately tackled and arrested.

the_arrest_decision.title: Phase 5: The Arrest
the_arrest_decision.description: The officer cuffs you anyway based on 'smell of alcohol' and 'glassy eyes.' You are placed in the cruiser. The officer starts asking: 'Where were you drinking tonight?'

choices.text: Remain completely silent.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: You are in custody. Anything you say now is an in-custody interrogation. Total silence is your strongest shield.

choices.text: Say: 'I want my lawyer.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Invoking your 6th Amendment right to counsel explicitly forces them to cease all questioning immediately.

choices.text: Say: 'I wasn't drinking, you're making a mistake.'
choices.feedback: WAIVER: Even denying guilt is engaging in the interrogation. If your story changes later, this initial statement hurts you.

choices.text: Insult the officer's intelligence and threaten their job.
choices.feedback: RISKY: Being verbally abusive will be noted in the police report and used to paint you as an aggressive, intoxicated defendant to the judge.

station_breathalyzer.title: Phase 6: The Station Breathalyzer
station_breathalyzer.description: At the station, they ask you to take the official Intoxilyzer 9000 test. They read you a form stating: 'If you refuse this test, your license will be suspended for 180 days.'

choices.text: Refuse the test, accepting the automatic 180-day license suspension.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL (For trial): Refusing the test means an automatic RMV suspension, but it denies the state the scientific evidence needed to convict you of the crime of OUI.

choices.text: Take the test, knowing you might blow over.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: If you blow over 0.08, you have just handed the prosecution a 'per se' guilty verdict. The short-term license save is usually not worth the criminal conviction.

choices.text: Demand to speak to a lawyer before deciding.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: In MA, you do not have a constitutional right to consult a lawyer *before* deciding to take the breathalyzer. They will mark it as a 'Refusal'.

choices.text: Pretend to blow but don't actually exhale into the tube.
choices.feedback: RISKY: The machine detects airflow. Officers will mark this as a 'Refusal' anyway, but document that you were being deceptive and uncooperative.

booking_process.title: Phase 7: The Booking Phone Call
booking_process.description: During booking, the officer points to a phone on the wall. 'You get a phone call. Make it quick.'

choices.text: Call a trusted contact to arrange bail and say: 'I was arrested, come get me. Don't ask questions on the phone.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Booking room phones are recorded. Keeping the conversation strictly to logistics prevents you from making recorded confessions.

choices.text: Call a lawyer and leave a voicemail with your basic info.
choices.feedback: SAFE: Good to call a lawyer, but you still need bail money. Calling a family member to handle logistics and hire the lawyer is usually faster at 2 AM.

choices.text: Call a friend and vent: 'I only had three drinks, I can't believe he arrested me.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just confessed to the crime on a recorded police line. The prosecutor will play this tape at your trial.

choices.text: Refuse the phone call.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: You have a statutory right to a phone call under MA law to arrange bail. Skipping it means staying in a cell longer.

victory.title: ENCOUNTER MASTERED
victory.description: You survived an OUI stop by strictly protecting your 5th Amendment rights and denying the state evidence. Your choices are in the Debrief Room.

possession_class_a.title: Possession Class A (Street Stop)
possession_class_a.description: Navigating a street detainment, the nuances of 'Terry Frisks', and illegal search and seizure tactics.

start.title: Phase 1: The Stop
start.description: You are walking down the street. A police cruiser pulls up onto the sidewalk blocking your path. Two officers step out. 'Hey, hold up a second. We got a report of drug activity around here. Where are you going?'

choices.text: Ask clearly: 'Am I being detained, or am I free to go?'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: This establishes whether this is a consensual encounter or a 4th Amendment seizure. If you aren't detained, you can legally walk away.

choices.text: Keep walking and ignore them completely.
choices.feedback: RISKY: While technically legal if it's consensual, ignoring officers who blocked your path usually triggers them to grab you and claim you 'fled.'

choices.text: Say: 'I'm just going to the store. I don't have any drugs on me.'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: You engaged in the investigation. By denying you have drugs before they specifically ask, you might actually raise their suspicion.

choices.text: Put your hands in your pockets and look away nervously.
choices.feedback: DANGEROUS: Reaching into concealed areas gives officers instant 'Reasonable Suspicion' that you have a weapon, justifying a physical takedown.

the_detainment.title: Phase 2: The Detainment
the_detainment.description: The officer says, 'You're detained for a minute while we figure this out. You match the description of a guy dealing heroin (Class A).'

choices.text: State: 'I invoke my 5th Amendment right to remain silent.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: You are officially detained. Shutting down the interrogation prevents them from tricking you into admitting guilt or giving contradictory statements.

choices.text: Ask: 'What was the exact description?'
choices.feedback: SAFE BUT RISKY: Probing for info is okay, but officers are legally allowed to lie to you about the description to get you to talk.

choices.text: Say: 'I don't sell heroin, I only have a little personal weed.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just confessed to possessing a different drug. Even if weed is legal, admitting to carrying narcotics justifies a full search of your person.

choices.text: Demand they call their supervisor immediately.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: You can ask for a supervisor, but it doesn't pause the current investigation or protect your rights in the moment.

the_search_request.title: Phase 3: The Search Request
the_search_request.description: The officer points at your pockets. 'If you don't have anything to hide, you won't mind if I check your pockets, right?'

choices.text: State loudly and clearly: 'I do not consent to any searches.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: This is your 4th Amendment shield. If they search you anyway without Probable Cause, any drugs they find will be thrown out in court.

choices.text: Say: 'No, I don't want you to.'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: It's a refusal, but 'I do not consent' is the explicit legal terminology courts look for on bodycam footage.

choices.text: Empty your pockets yourself to show them you are clean.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: Pulling things out of your pocket is considered 'voluntary consent' to a search. You lose your 4th Amendment protections.

choices.text: Say: 'Go ahead.'
choices.feedback: WAIVER: You just gave full consent. They can now legally search your pockets, your bag, and your shoes.

the_terry_frisk.title: Phase 4: The 'Terry' Frisk
the_terry_frisk.description: Despite your refusal, the officer says, 'I'm patting you down for weapons for my safety.' He pats outside your jeans and feels a small, soft plastic baggie. 'What's this bump?'

choices.text: Remain completely silent.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Under the 'Plain Feel Doctrine', an officer can only seize an item during a pat-down if it is *immediately apparent* it's contraband. A soft baggie isn't a weapon. Silence forces them to make an illegal leap.

choices.text: Say: 'It's just candy.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: If it's not candy, you just lied to an officer. Silence is always better than a fabricated cover story.

choices.text: Say: 'You said you were looking for weapons, that's not a weapon.'
choices.feedback: SAFE: This correctly points out the legal limit of a Terry Frisk, but arguing with the officer on the street rarely works. Silence is safer.

choices.text: Say: 'It's not mine, I'm holding it for a friend!'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just admitted you know it's contraband. 'Constructive Possession' means you are guilty even if you don't own it.

the_seizure.title: Phase 5: The Illegal Seizure
the_seizure.description: The officer reaches inside your pocket anyway (violating the Terry Frisk scope) and pulls out the baggie of Class A narcotics. 'Looks like you're under arrest.'

choices.text: Do not resist physically. Maintain silence. Memorize badge numbers.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: You survive the street encounter. You will easily win the 'Motion to Suppress' in court because you clearly stated 'I do not consent' and he illegally reached into your pocket.

choices.text: Yell: 'That's an illegal search! Put it back!'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: You are right, but arguing won't un-arrest you. The courtroom is where you fight illegal searches, not the sidewalk.

choices.text: Try to grab the baggie out of his hand.
choices.feedback: DANGEROUS: This is Assault on a Police Officer and Destruction of Evidence. You will face years in prison regardless of the illegal search.

choices.text: Run away.
choices.feedback: DANGEROUS: Fleeing turns an illegal search case you would have won into a Resisting Arrest/Fleeing charge.

miranda_rights.title: Phase 6: Miranda Tactics
miranda_rights.description: While cuffing you, the officer doesn't read you your Miranda rights. Instead, he asks casually on the way to the cruiser: 'Who sold this to you? Tell me and I'll let you go.'

choices.text: Say: 'I want a lawyer.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Miranda is only required *before* in-custody interrogation. By asking you questions in cuffs without reading them, he is breaking rules. Invoking counsel shuts it down.

choices.text: Give him the name of a local dealer to get out of trouble.
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: Police lie. He won't let you go. You just confessed to possessing the drugs and buying them, cementing their case.

choices.text: Say: 'You didn't read me my rights!'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Never educate the police on their mistakes. Let your lawyer use the Miranda failure to get your statements thrown out later.

choices.text: Start crying and begging for leniency.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: Emotional breakdowns often lead to accidental confessions. Stay calm and stay quiet.

booking_defense.title: Phase 7: Station Questioning
booking_defense.description: At the station, detectives try one last time. 'Look, it's just a possession charge. Sign this paper saying the drugs are yours, and we'll recommend bail.'

choices.text: Refuse to sign anything without an attorney present.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Never sign police documents without a lawyer. Bail recommendations are made by the bail commissioner, not the arresting detective.

choices.text: Read the paper carefully to see if it's fair.
choices.feedback: RISKY: If you aren't a lawyer, you don't know what rights you are waiving by reading their paperwork. Just refuse.

choices.text: Sign it so you can go home tonight.
choices.feedback: FATAL MISTAKE: You just signed a written confession. The trial is effectively over, and you will be convicted of Class A Possession.

choices.text: Ask if you can cross out certain lines before signing.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Trying to play lawyer with a detective is a losing game. Absolute refusal to sign is the only safe move.

victory.title: ENCOUNTER MASTERED
victory.description: You survived a street search and laid the groundwork for your lawyer to get the case dismissed based on an illegal 4th Amendment seizure.

assault_battery.title: Assault & Battery (Investigation)
assault_battery.description: Handling police arriving at your home, avoiding accidental confessions, and navigating self-defense claims.

start.title: Phase 1: The Knock at the Door
start.description: You had a physical altercation with someone earlier. Now, police are knocking on your front door. 'Boston Police! Open up, we just want to get your side of the story.'

choices.text: Step outside, close the door behind you, and ask: 'How can I help you?'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: You address the police without giving them visual or physical access to the inside of your home (protecting your 4th Amendment sanctuary).

choices.text: Open the door wide and say: 'Come on in, officers.'
choices.feedback: WAIVER: You just gave full consent to enter your home. Anything illegal they see in 'plain view' is now grounds for arrest, and you lose home court advantage.

choices.text: Talk to them through a cracked window or doorbell camera.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Using physical barriers completely protects you from 'exigent circumstances' entries and prevents them from pushing their way in.

choices.text: Pretend you aren't home and ignore the knocking.
choices.feedback: RISKY: While legal, if they have Probable Cause, ignoring them might lead them to kick the door down claiming 'fleeing suspect'.

the_story_trap.title: Phase 2: The 'Side of the Story' Trap
the_story_trap.description: The officer says, 'The other guy is in the hospital. He says you started it. If you just tell us you hit him in self-defense, we can wrap this up.'

choices.text: State: 'I will not answer any questions without my lawyer present.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: The officer is using a classic interrogation tactic. To claim self-defense, you first have to admit you hit him. If you admit it, they arrest you for A&B.

choices.text: Say: 'He swung first! I was just defending myself.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just confessed to the act of Battery. The police don't decide self-defense; a judge does. You just bought yourself an arrest.

choices.text: Say: 'I don't know what you're talking about, I wasn't there.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: If they have video or witnesses proving you were there, lying to the police becomes 'consciousness of guilt' evidence at trial.

choices.text: Say: 'I'll write down my statement for you.'
choices.feedback: WAIVER: Putting a statement in writing without a lawyer guarantees you will lock yourself into a story that prosecutors will pick apart.

visual_inspection.title: Phase 3: Visual Inspection
visual_inspection.description: Since you won't talk, the officer looks at your hands. 'Your knuckles are bruised and bleeding. How did that happen?'

choices.text: Put your hands in your pockets and remain silent.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: You have no obligation to explain your injuries to the police. Silence protects you from making an incriminating statement.

choices.text: Say: 'I fell and scraped them on the pavement.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: Again, fabricating a cover story is dangerous. If forensic analysis shows DNA on your knuckles, your lie proves guilt.

choices.text: Say: 'That's from when I punched him in self-defense.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You re-confirmed the battery. You will be placed in handcuffs immediately.

choices.text: Ask: 'Do you have a warrant to look at my hands?'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: They don't need a warrant to look at things in 'plain view'. Putting your hands away is the legal way to end the visual inspection.

the_threat_arrest.title: Phase 4: The Arrest Threat
the_threat_arrest.description: The officer pulls out handcuffs. 'If you don't cooperate and tell me what happened right now, I'm taking you in for Aggravated Assault.'

choices.text: Say: 'I understand, but I still want my lawyer.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: They are trying to scare you into talking. If they had enough Probable Cause to arrest you, they would have done it already without asking.

choices.text: Panic and tell them everything to avoid jail.
choices.feedback: FATAL MISTAKE: Fear is their weapon. Confessing guarantees you go to jail. Silence leaves them without enough evidence.

choices.text: Say: 'You can't arrest me, I know my rights.'
choices.feedback: SAFE BUT ANTAGONISTIC: Never argue law with a cop on the street. It provokes them. Stick to invoking your rights clearly and politely.

choices.text: Step backward into your house and try to lock the door.
choices.feedback: DANGER: If they are mid-arrest, stepping back into the house is 'fleeing', which allows them to break the door down under 'hot pursuit'.

the_detention.title: Phase 5: The Arrest
the_detention.description: They arrest you anyway based on the bruised knuckles and the victim's statement. At the station, they put you in an interrogation room and offer you water. 'Just a friendly chat off the record.'

choices.text: Say: 'I want my attorney. I will not speak until they are present.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: There is NO SUCH THING as 'off the record' in a police station. Re-invoke your 6th Amendment right immediately to stop all questioning.

choices.text: Take the water and engage in small talk.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: Small talk is a rapport-building tactic used to make you comfortable enough to slip up and admit details about the crime.

choices.text: Say: 'I'll talk off the record, but you can't use it in court.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: They absolutely can and will use it in court. Police are legally allowed to lie to you about what is admissible.

choices.text: Refuse the water and stare at the wall.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Silence is good, but explicitly stating 'I want a lawyer' forces them legally to leave the room. Staring allows them to keep pressuring you.

good_cop.title: Phase 6: Good Cop Routine
good_cop.description: A new detective enters. 'Hey man, the first guy was a jerk. I believe you. The other guy has a record. If you just write down that he attacked you, I can get you out of here.'

choices.text: Maintain your demand for a lawyer and stay silent.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: The 'Good Cop' is just as dangerous. They are still trying to get you to admit you were involved in the physical altercation.

choices.text: Write down the statement claiming self-defense.
choices.feedback: FATAL MISTAKE: You fell for it. You provided a written confession of Battery. The prosecutor will use it to convict you.

choices.text: Tell him verbally what happened but refuse to write it.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: Verbal confessions are just as admissible as written ones. The detective will testify to everything you said.

choices.text: Ask the 'Good Cop' for legal advice on what to do.
choices.feedback: RISKY: Police are not your lawyers. Their 'advice' will always be designed to help them close the case by securing your confession.

booking.title: Phase 7: The Jailhouse Informant
booking.description: You are placed in a holding cell. Another inmate strikes up a conversation. 'Man, what are you in for? I'm in for a bar fight. Did you hit somebody?'

choices.text: Say: 'I don't want to talk about my case.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Undercover cops or informants are frequently placed in holding cells. Never discuss your case with anyone except your lawyer.

choices.text: Say: 'Yeah, some guy tried me and I had to handle it.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just confessed to a potential informant. They will trade your confession to the prosecutor for a lighter sentence on their own case.

choices.text: Ask the inmate for details about their fight.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: While you aren't talking about your case, engaging in conversation opens the door for them to keep pressing you.

choices.text: Lie and say you are in for traffic tickets.
choices.feedback: RISKY: Lies can complicate things. Simply refusing to discuss it is the safest, most professional boundary.

victory.title: ENCOUNTER MASTERED
victory.description: You avoided every interrogation trap. By maintaining silence, you gave your defense attorney a clean slate to argue self-defense or lack of evidence in court.

possession_wi_distribute_class_b.title: Possession W/ Intent to Distribute (Class B)
possession_wi_distribute_class_b.description: Navigating a vehicle search, the 'Intent' trap, and attempts to search your cell phone for distribution evidence.

start.title: Phase 1: The Pretextual Stop
start.description: You are pulled over for a 'broken taillight.' The BPD officer approaches, looks in your backseat, and spots a digital scale and a roll of sandwich bags. 'What are you doing with those baggies and scale?'

choices.text: State clearly: 'I decline to answer any questions without an attorney.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: You immediately invoke your 5th Amendment right. Paraphernalia in plain view gives them reasonable suspicion, but your silence denies them the confession needed to prove 'intent'.

choices.text: Say: 'I use the scale to weigh my own food for my diet.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: Fabricating a cover story is dangerous. If they find drug residue on the scale later, your lie will be used as evidence of 'consciousness of guilt.'

choices.text: Say: 'I just buy in bulk for me and my friends to save money.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just confessed to 'Distribution.' In Massachusetts, sharing or buying for friends—even without profit—legally constitutes Distribution.

choices.text: Ask: 'Is it illegal to own sandwich bags?'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: While true that bags aren't illegal, arguing with the officer just prolongs the stop. Silence is always safer.

the_vehicle_search.title: Phase 2: The Vehicle Search
the_vehicle_search.description: The officer says, 'Based on the scale and bags, I have Probable Cause to search this car. Step out.'

choices.text: Step out, lock the door behind you, and state: 'I do not consent to this search.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: If they search anyway and find narcotics, stating your non-consent on bodycam preserves your right to challenge the 'Probable Cause' in court later.

choices.text: Say: 'Go ahead, search it. I have nothing to hide.'
choices.feedback: FATAL WAIVER: You gave 'voluntary consent.' Even if your lawyer could have proven the search was illegal, your consent waives your 4th Amendment rights completely.

choices.text: Refuse to step out of the car.
choices.feedback: DANGER: Under Pennsylvania v. Mimms, police can legally order you out of a vehicle during a stop. Refusing leads to forced extraction and a Resisting Arrest charge.

choices.text: Step out and leave the doors wide open for them.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: You complied, but you didn't explicitly state 'I do not consent,' which makes fighting the search in court slightly harder.

the_discovery.title: Phase 3: The Discovery & The Cash
the_discovery.description: During the search, they find 5 grams of a Class B substance (e.g., Cocaine/Adderall) and $400 in cash in your pocket. '5 grams and all this cash? You're definitely dealing. Who do you sell to?'

choices.text: Remain completely silent.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: The combination of drugs, cash, and scales gives them circumstantial evidence of Intent to Distribute (PWID). Silence prevents you from confirming their theory.

choices.text: Say: 'That's my rent money, and the drugs are just for my personal use!'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just confessed to Possession of Class B. You handed them a guaranteed conviction on the lesser charge.

choices.text: Say: 'I don't know whose drugs those are, this isn't my car.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: If it's your car or they find your fingerprints on the bag, this lie destroys your credibility. Silence is better than a lie.

choices.text: Demand they give you your cash back immediately.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: They will seize the cash under Civil Asset Forfeiture laws, suspecting it is drug proceeds. Arguing now won't get it back.

phone_seizure.title: Phase 4: The Phone Seizure
phone_seizure.description: The officer pulls your cell phone from the center console. 'Unlock this phone for me. If you're just a user, there won't be any text messages about selling, right?'

choices.text: State clearly: 'I do not consent to a search of my phone. I want my lawyer.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Under Riley v. California, police absolutely need a warrant to search a cell phone. Refusing to unlock it protects your private data.

choices.text: Unlock the phone to prove you don't sell drugs.
choices.feedback: FATAL WAIVER: You consented. Even if you don't sell, an old text saying 'can you bring me some?' can be twisted by prosecutors into a distribution conspiracy.

choices.text: Say: 'I forgot my passcode.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: Lying is always a bad idea. Simply asserting your 4th Amendment right not to unlock it is legal and much safer.

choices.text: Try to snatch the phone back out of his hands.
choices.feedback: DANGER: This is Assault and Battery on a Police Officer. You will be violently detained.

miranda_interrogation.title: Phase 5: The PWID Trap
miranda_interrogation.description: At the station, a detective reads your Miranda rights. 'Listen, we're charging you with Possession with Intent to Distribute. That carries heavy jail time. Just tell me you only sell to support your own habit, and I'll talk to the DA.'

choices.text: Say: 'I am invoking my right to remain silent and I want my attorney.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: The 'I only sell to support my habit' line is a classic trap. It sounds sympathetic, but it's a full legal confession to the felony of Distribution.

choices.text: Say: 'I don't sell to anyone! It was all for me.'
choices.feedback: WAIVER: You just admitted to Possession again. You must stop talking to prevent them from building their case.

choices.text: Agree with the detective to get a lighter sentence.
choices.feedback: FATAL WAIVER: Detectives cannot promise lighter sentences; only the DA and judge do that. You just gave them a taped confession to a felony.

choices.text: Ask: 'What kind of deal can the DA offer me?'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: You are trying to negotiate without a lawyer. This often leads to accidental admissions. Let your attorney negotiate deals.

confidential_informant.title: Phase 6: The C.I. Offer
confidential_informant.description: The detective changes tactics. 'Okay, how about this. Give me the name of your supplier. Help us catch a bigger fish, and we'll make this whole thing disappear. You want to be a Confidential Informant?'

choices.text: Say nothing. Wait for your lawyer to handle any cooperation agreements.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Never become a C.I. without an attorney negotiating an 'immunity agreement' in writing first. Police will often use your info and charge you anyway.

choices.text: Give them the name of the person you bought it from.
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just confessed to purchasing Class B narcotics without getting any written guarantee of immunity.

choices.text: Say: 'I don't know anyone, I found it on the street.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: Another fabricated story that locks you in. Silence is the only answer.

choices.text: Say: 'I'll do it if you let me go right now.'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: You are signaling you are guilty and willing to talk, but without a lawyer present to draft the contract, you have zero leverage.

booking_bail.title: Phase 7: Booking & Bail
booking_bail.description: The detective gives up and sends you to booking. The Bail Commissioner arrives and asks: 'Do you have a job? How are you going to pay for this cash bail?'

choices.text: Answer truthfully about your employment and residency to secure low bail.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: The Bail Commissioner is not investigating the crime; they only determine flight risk. Providing proof of employment and local ties helps get you released.

choices.text: Say: 'I make plenty of money from my side hustle.'
choices.feedback: FATAL MISTAKE: Implication of illegal income will cause the commissioner to raise your bail or deny it, as drug money makes you a flight risk.

choices.text: Refuse to speak to the Bail Commissioner.
choices.feedback: RISKY: While you shouldn't discuss the case, refusing to answer basic biographical questions will likely result in being held in jail until arraignment.

choices.text: Lie about your address so the police can't find you later.
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL: Giving a false address during booking is a crime and guarantees your bail will be revoked when they find out.

victory.title: ENCOUNTER MASTERED
victory.description: You successfully defended against the 'Intent to Distribute' traps. By maintaining silence, your lawyer can argue this was merely personal possession.

shoplifting_50_to_200.title: Shoplifting ($50 to $200)
shoplifting_50_to_200.description: Dealing with Store Loss Prevention (LP), 'Shopkeeper's Privilege', and avoiding civil demand traps.

start.title: Phase 1: The LP Intercept
start.description: You are exiting a retail store. A plainclothes Loss Prevention (LP) employee steps in front of you. 'Excuse me, store security. I need you to come back inside with me to discuss some unpaid merchandise in your bag.'

choices.text: Ask calmly: 'Am I legally detained, or am I free to leave?'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: LP officers are private citizens. In MA, they have 'Shopkeeper's Privilege' to detain you if they have reasonable grounds, but establishing whether you are actually detained is step one.

choices.text: Say: 'I didn't steal anything!' and try to push past them.
choices.feedback: DANGEROUS: Pushing an LP officer turns a simple misdemeanor shoplifting charge into Unarmed Robbery or Assault and Battery.

choices.text: Ignore them completely and keep walking.
choices.feedback: RISKY: While they aren't police, if they have grounds, ignoring them often leads to them physically grabbing you or calling police to intercept you outside.

choices.text: Say: 'I'll just pay for it right now, here is my card.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: Offering to pay for hidden items is a confession of shoplifting. They will still detain you and now have a confession.

the_back_room.title: Phase 2: The Back Room
the_back_room.description: You comply and are taken to the LP office. The LP officer says, 'Empty your bag on the table. If you cooperate, we might not call the police.'

choices.text: State: 'I do not consent to a search of my property, and I will not answer questions.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: LP officers cannot legally force you to empty your bag without your consent. They can hold you until police arrive, but you should not assist their investigation.

choices.text: Empty your bag to show you are cooperating.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: You just handed them the evidence. LP will use this to call the police and show them the recovered merchandise.

choices.text: Say: 'You have no right to hold me here, this is kidnapping!'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: MA law allows reasonable detainment by merchants. Arguing 'kidnapping' is factually incorrect and escalates the tension.

choices.text: Try to grab your bag and run out the back door.
choices.feedback: DANGEROUS: Fleeing from a lawful LP detainment guarantees police will be called, upgrading the severity of the charges.

the_civil_demand.title: Phase 3: The Civil Demand Notice
the_civil_demand.description: The LP officer slides a piece of paper across the desk. 'Sign this trespass notice and this Admission of Guilt form. It says you'll pay the store $500 for our trouble, and you can leave.'

choices.text: Refuse to sign the Admission of Guilt or the Civil Demand.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Never sign an LP confession form. It is a legally binding admission that will be handed directly to the police and prosecutors.

choices.text: Sign it so you can go home immediately.
choices.feedback: FATAL MISTAKE: They will likely call the police anyway, and now you have signed a written confession. You also just agreed to an exorbitant civil fine.

choices.text: Sign a fake name on the paper.
choices.feedback: RISKY: If police arrive and identify you, signing a false name can lead to additional charges like Forgery or Uttering.

choices.text: Ask: 'If I sign this, do you promise not to call the cops?'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: LP officers frequently lie. They have no legal obligation to keep their promise, and they usually call BPD regardless.

police_arrival.title: Phase 4: BPD Arrives
police_arrival.description: BPD officers arrive. The LP employee tells them, 'He was concealing items in his bag, but he refused to show us.' The police officer turns to you: 'Is that true? What's in the bag?'

choices.text: State: 'I invoke my right to remain silent and I want a lawyer.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: The police are now conducting a criminal investigation. Treat them exactly as you would on the street—absolute silence.

choices.text: Say: 'They are lying, I didn't steal anything!'
choices.feedback: WAIVER: You are engaging in the interrogation. If they do find items in your bag, your lie will be used against you in court.

choices.text: Say: 'I just forgot to pay for it, it was an accident.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just admitted to removing the items past the point of sale. Intent can be hard to prove, but a confession makes it easy.

choices.text: Blame the LP officer for harassing you.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Deflecting doesn't protect your rights. The police only care if you have unpaid merchandise on you.

the_police_search.title: Phase 5: The Police Search
the_police_search.description: The police officer says, 'Well, store security has cameras. I'm going to search your bag right now.'

choices.text: State clearly: 'I do not consent to a search of my property.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Force the officer to rely on 'Probable Cause' (the LP's testimony/video). If the LP was wrong, stating non-consent allows your lawyer to suppress the search later.

choices.text: Open the bag for the officer.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: Voluntary consent destroys your 4th Amendment protections. Never assist in your own search.

choices.text: Hold the bag tightly against your chest.
choices.feedback: DANGEROUS: Physical resistance against a police officer will result in you being tackled and charged with Resisting Arrest.

choices.text: Say: 'You need a warrant for that!'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: They likely have Probable Cause based on the LP's report, which provides an exception to the warrant requirement. Just state 'I do not consent.'

the_summons.title: Phase 6: The Summons Decision
the_summons.description: The officers find $100 worth of items. Because it is a misdemeanor and you have ID, the officer says, 'I'm not bringing you to jail today. I'm giving you a summons to appear in court.'

choices.text: Accept the paperwork, remain silent, and leave the store.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: A 'Summons/Clerk Magistrate Hearing' is a huge win. You go home instead of jail. Don't say anything that ruins this outcome.

choices.text: Argue with the officer that you shouldn't get a summons.
choices.feedback: RISKY: A summons is a courtesy. If you argue and become 'disorderly', they will rip up the summons and arrest you on the spot.

choices.text: Apologize profusely to the officer and the store manager.
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: An apology is an admission of guilt. This can be used against you at the Magistrate hearing.

choices.text: Refuse to take the paperwork from the officer.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Refusing the paper doesn't magically make the court date disappear; it just means you might miss court and get a warrant for your arrest.

trespass_notice.title: Phase 7: The Trespass Warning
trespass_notice.description: As you leave, the LP manager hands you a 'No Trespass' order. 'If you ever come back to any of our locations, you will be arrested instantly for trespassing.'

choices.text: Take the notice and never return to that store chain.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Private businesses can ban anyone they want. Violating a trespass order is an entirely separate, easily provable crime.

choices.text: Throw the notice on the ground and walk out.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Littering/acting aggressive right in front of the cops who just gave you a break is a bad idea. Take the paper and leave.

choices.text: Refuse to accept the paper.
choices.feedback: RISKY: They don't need your signature or acceptance to ban you. Verbal notice in front of police is legally binding.

choices.text: Say: 'I'll shop here if I want to!'
choices.feedback: RISKY: Antagonizing them as you leave shows a lack of respect for the trespass order, practically begging police to re-engage.

victory.title: ENCOUNTER MASTERED
victory.description: You navigated the murky waters of Store Loss Prevention. By not signing confessions and maintaining silence with BPD, you minimized the damage.

shoplifting_200_over.title: Shoplifting ($200 & Over)
shoplifting_200_over.description: Facing higher-tier shoplifting/larceny accusations, aggressive Loss Prevention tactics, and the 'Value' trap.

start.title: Phase 1: The Alarm & The Block
start.description: You walk through the exit sensors and the alarm blares. Two Loss Prevention (LP) officers immediately physically block your path. 'We watched you conceal those electronics. That's over $300 worth of merchandise. Come with us now.'

choices.text: Comply, keep your hands visible, and say: 'I will walk with you, but I am not answering questions.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: High-value shoplifting often makes LP more aggressive. Complying physically prevents 'Robbery' escalation, while immediate silence protects you legally.

choices.text: Try to push through them to get out the door.
choices.feedback: DANGER: Using physical force to escape with stolen goods elevates Shoplifting to 'Unarmed Robbery'—a serious felony.

choices.text: Drop the bag and run away.
choices.feedback: RISKY: Leaving the goods might prevent a Robbery charge, but they have you on camera. Running guarantees police will hunt for you based on your description.

choices.text: Yell: 'The sensors are broken, I didn't take anything!'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Arguing in public creates a scene. If they have you on camera concealing items, lying only hurts your credibility.

the_value_trap.title: Phase 2: The 'Value' Interrogation
the_value_trap.description: In the back room, the LP manager starts pulling electronics out of your bag. 'This is an iPad and headphones. That's easily $600. You're looking at a felony Larceny charge.'

choices.text: Remain completely silent.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: The value of the items determines the severity of the charge. Never agree with their valuation or admit you knew how much it was worth.

choices.text: Say: 'No way, those were on sale for $150 total!'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just admitted to stealing them, and you are just arguing over the price. You handed them a confession.

choices.text: Say: 'I didn't know they were that expensive.'
choices.feedback: WAIVER: Another admission of guilt disguised as an excuse. Silence is the only safe option.

choices.text: Ask: 'Can I just pay for them now and leave?'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: Offering to pay after being caught is a confession. LP is not a cashier; they will take your confession and call the police.

the_police_threat.title: Phase 3: The Threat
the_police_threat.description: The LP officer says, 'Look, if you sign this form admitting you took it, we will just ban you from the store. If you don't, I'm calling BPD and telling them you assaulted me.'

choices.text: State: 'I will not sign anything. I want my lawyer.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Never give in to extortion. LP officers frequently threaten extra charges to get a signed confession. Stand your ground.

choices.text: Sign the form to avoid the fake assault charge.
choices.feedback: FATAL MISTAKE: You signed a confession for a high-value theft. They will call the police anyway and hand them the signed paper.

choices.text: Call their bluff: 'Go ahead, call the cops then.'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: While calling their bluff is correct, engaging in a verbal spar with LP is unnecessary. Silence is more professional and safer.

choices.text: Pull out your phone and start recording the LP officer.
choices.feedback: RISKY: You are on private property. The store can legally demand you stop recording and hit you with trespassing.

police_escalation.title: Phase 4: BPD Arrives
police_escalation.description: BPD arrives. The LP hands them the $600 worth of items. The BPD officer says to you: 'Over $250 means we're putting you in cuffs. Why'd you take all this stuff?'

choices.text: State: 'I am invoking my right to remain silent.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: You are facing a serious charge. The police are trying to lock down your motive to finalize their report. Say nothing.

choices.text: Say: 'I lost my job and I really needed the money.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: Confessing your motive seals the case. Sympathy rarely works on the street; it just provides prosecutors with evidence of 'Intent'.

choices.text: Say: 'I thought my friend already paid for it.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: A weak cover story that can be easily disproven by the store's security cameras. Never lie.

choices.text: Beg the officer to give you a summons instead of arresting you.
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Begging rarely works for higher-value thefts. You are better off saving your energy for the booking process.

miranda_car.title: Phase 5: The Cruiser Confession Trap
miranda_car.description: You are handcuffed and placed in the cruiser. The officer doesn't read you Miranda rights. As he drives, he asks casually: 'Man, you must have a buyer for all those electronics, right?'

choices.text: Say: 'I want my lawyer.'
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: This is a classic tactic. By not reading Miranda, he hopes you'll talk casually. Invoking your right to counsel shuts this down.

choices.text: Say: 'No, they were just for me.'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You avoided a 'Distribution' implication but confessed to the theft. They will argue the statement was 'spontaneous' and admissible.

choices.text: Say: 'You didn't read me my rights, I'm not talking!'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Never educate the police. Just invoke your rights. Let your lawyer use the Miranda failure in court later.

choices.text: Stay silent, but nod your head 'no'.
choices.feedback: WAIVER: Body language is communication. Nodding 'no' is an answer to an investigatory question.

booking_inventory.title: Phase 6: The Booking Inventory
booking_inventory.description: During the booking process at the station, they empty your pockets and find a pair of wire cutters. The officer says: 'Ah, so you brought tools to cut the security tags off?'

choices.text: Remain completely silent.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Possessing a tool used to defeat security devices can lead to an additional charge of 'Possession of a Burglarious Instrument'. Silence is imperative.

choices.text: Say: 'I use those for my job as an electrician.'
choices.feedback: RISKY: Even if true, you are engaging in the interrogation. Let your lawyer explain your tools to the judge later.

choices.text: Say: 'I didn't even use them in the store!'
choices.feedback: CRIMINAL WAIVER: You just implied you brought them for that purpose but didn't get around to it. You are handing them more charges.

choices.text: Demand to call your lawyer immediately.
choices.feedback: SAFE: You have a right to a phone call, but the booking process (fingerprinting/inventory) usually has to finish first. Silence is the immediate priority.

the_bail_commissioner.title: Phase 7: The Final Hurdles
the_bail_commissioner.description: Because of the high value of the items, the Bail Commissioner is setting a cash bail. They ask: 'Have you ever been arrested for theft or larceny before?'

choices.text: Answer truthfully. They have your record right in front of them anyway.
choices.feedback: OPTIMAL: Never lie about your criminal record to a Bail Commissioner. They run a CORI check. Lying destroys your credibility and guarantees higher bail.

choices.text: Lie and say 'No, this is my first time.'
choices.feedback: DANGEROUS: They will see your record on the computer immediately. You will be denied bail for being untrustworthy.

choices.text: Refuse to answer.
choices.feedback: RISKY: The Bail Commissioner controls your immediate freedom. Being totally uncooperative with basic background questions usually means staying in a cell.

choices.text: Say: 'Yes, but those were misunderstandings.'
choices.feedback: SUB-OPTIMAL: Don't make excuses for past records. Just answer 'yes' or 'no' truthfully so they can process your release.

victory.title: ENCOUNTER MASTERED
victory.description: You survived a high-stakes shoplifting/larceny arrest. By refusing to sign the LP forms and maintaining silence, you prevented them from upgrading the charges with your own words.
